content.text: A demand intake portal is the structured entry point through which teams across an enterprise submit requests for new agentic AI capabilities. Without a standardised intake process, organisations end up with shadow AI projects, duplicate development efforts, and no visibility into the aggregate demand on shared infrastructure. A well-designed portal makes demand visible, comparable, and prioritisable.

attrs.alt: Demand intake portal five-stage pipeline with SLA targets

content.text: Why a structured intake process matters

Agentic AI programmes attract demand from every corner of the enterprise simultaneously. Sales wants an AI that automates proposal drafting; operations wants an agent for invoice processing; customer service wants an automated resolution bot. Without a structured intake process, these requests compete informally — the loudest stakeholder wins, not the highest-value initiative.
Visibility: a portal makes all AI requests visible to the programme management function, enabling portfolio-level prioritisation rather than reactive accommodation.
Comparability: a standard submission form forces requestors to provide enough information (business value, estimated users, data sensitivity) for apples-to-apples comparison.
Auditability: every request is time-stamped, categorised, and tracked through to its outcome — approved, declined, or deferred — providing a complete audit trail.
Resource planning: aggregate intake data reveals demand patterns that inform hiring, commitment sizing, and platform capacity planning.

The intake form is the intake portal's core artefact. It must be concise enough that requestors complete it willingly, yet comprehensive enough for the triage team to make an informed decision without back-and-forth. Eight to twelve fields is the practical maximum.
Revenue, cost saving, risk reduction, CX
PII, confidential, public — affects risk tier
Team budget, shared pool, or TBD

Keep the form in a system that sends automated acknowledgement on submission, assigns a tracking ID, and routes to the triage queue automatically. Jira Service Management, ServiceNow, and Notion forms with Zapier automation are all viable depending on your stack.

The first triage pass should be automated. It checks for completeness, detects potential duplicates against existing portfolio items, assigns a category label (customer-facing, internal ops, infrastructure, data pipeline), and computes a preliminary risk tier. This happens within one hour of submission.

import dataclasses, datetime, re
from enum import Enum
from typing import Optional

class DataSensitivity(Enum):
    PUBLIC = "public"
    INTERNAL = "internal"
    CONFIDENTIAL = "confidential"
    PII = "pii"
    REGULATED = "regulated"

class RiskTier(Enum):
    LOW = "low"          # public data, internal tools only
    MEDIUM = "medium"    # confidential data or customer-facing
    HIGH = "high"        # PII, regulated data, or autonomous decisions
    CRITICAL = "critical"  # regulated data + autonomous decisions + high volume

@dataclasses.dataclass
class IntakeRequest:
    request_id: str
    title: str
    problem_statement: str
    business_value_type: str   # "revenue" | "cost_saving" | "risk_reduction" | "cx"
    annual_value_usd: float
    target_user_count: int
    data_sensitivity: DataSensitivity
    desired_date: datetime.date
    requestor: str
    sponsor: Optional[str]
    submitted_at: datetime.datetime = dataclasses.field(default_factory=datetime.datetime.utcnow)
    category: Optional[str] = None
    risk_tier: Optional[RiskTier] = None
    duplicate_of: Optional[str] = None
    triage_status: str = "pending"   # pending | triaged | human_review | decided

    def is_complete(self) -> tuple[bool, list[str]]:
        missing = []
        if not self.title.strip():
            missing.append("title")
        if not self.problem_statement.strip():
            missing.append("problem_statement")
        if self.annual_value_usd <= 0:
            missing.append("annual_value_usd")
        if self.target_user_count <= 0:
            missing.append("target_user_count")
        if not self.requestor.strip():
            missing.append("requestor")
        return len(missing) == 0, missing

    def compute_risk_tier(self) -> RiskTier:
        if self.data_sensitivity in (DataSensitivity.REGULATED, DataSensitivity.PII):
            return RiskTier.HIGH if self.target_user_count < 10_000 else RiskTier.CRITICAL
        if self.data_sensitivity == DataSensitivity.CONFIDENTIAL:
            return RiskTier.MEDIUM
        return RiskTier.LOW

    def auto_categorise(self) -> str:
        kw = (self.title + " " + self.problem_statement).lower()
        if any(w in kw for w in ["customer", "user", "client", "support", "sales"]):
            return "customer-facing"
        if any(w in kw for w in ["invoice", "finance", "hr", "operations", "workflow"]):
            return "internal-ops"
        if any(w in kw for w in ["data", "pipeline", "etl", "analytics", "reporting"]):
            return "data-pipeline"
        return "infrastructure"

    def run_auto_triage(self) -> None:
        complete, missing = self.is_complete()
        if not complete:
            self.triage_status = "incomplete"
            return
        self.risk_tier = self.compute_risk_tier()
        self.category = self.auto_categorise()
        self.triage_status = "triaged"

Duplicate requests are common — teams often submit requests without knowing that another team has already raised a similar initiative. Automated duplicate detection compares new submissions against the existing portfolio using title similarity and keyword overlap.

When a potential duplicate is detected, the auto-triage step flags it and notifies both the original requestor and the new requestor. A human reviewer then decides whether to merge the requests, link them as related, or treat them as genuinely distinct.

After human review confirms feasibility and scope, each request is scored using a weighted formula that balances business value, strategic alignment, implementation effort, and risk. The score determines which priority tier the request enters.
The scoring system maps each request to one of four priority tiers. Each tier has a defined SLA, routing path, and resource commitment level.
P1 requests bypass the standard queue and receive a named sponsor from senior leadership — without a sponsor, a P1 designation is meaningless.
P4 declines must include a written rationale explaining why the request was deferred and what conditions would allow re-submission.
Scores are not final: a P3 request can be fast-tracked if a critical dependency makes it a blocker for a P1 initiative.
Requestors deserve a clear, timely decision on every submission. A decision template ensures consistency and reduces the volume of status-check queries received by the programme management team.

DECISION_TEMPLATE = """
Subject: AI Initiative Request {request_id} — Decision: {decision}

Hi {requestor_name},

Thank you for submitting "{title}" to the AI programme intake portal.

**Decision: {decision}**
**Priority Tier: {priority_tier}**
**Composite Score: {score:.2f}/10**

{rationale}

**Next Steps:**
{next_steps}

**Key Contacts:**
- Programme Manager: {pm_name} <{pm_email}>
- Technical Review Lead: {tech_lead}

Questions? Reply to this email or comment on {ticket_url}.

The AI Programme Management Office
"""

def render_decision_email(
    scored: ScoredRequest,
    pm_name: str,
    pm_email: str,
    tech_lead: str,
    ticket_url: str,
) -> str:
    tier = scored.priority_tier
    if tier == "P1-Fast-Track":
        decision = "Approved — Fast Track"
        rationale = (
            "Your initiative scored in the top tier and will receive a dedicated squad. "
            "A sponsor will reach out within 5 business days to kick off discovery."
        )
        next_steps = (
            "1. Sponsor introduction meeting (within 5 BD)\n"
            "2. Discovery workshop to refine scope (within 2 weeks)\n"
            "3. Squad assigned and sprint planning begins (within 3 weeks)"
        )
    elif tier == "P2-Next-Cohort":
        decision = "Approved — Next Quarterly Cohort"
        rationale = (
            "Your initiative is approved and scheduled for the next programme cohort. "
            "Capacity will be confirmed at the upcoming quarterly planning session."
        )
        next_steps = (
            "1. You will receive cohort confirmation within 30 days\n"
            "2. Discovery workshop scheduled 4 weeks before cohort start\n"
            "3. Monthly update emails until cohort begins"
        )
    else:
        decision = "Deferred"
        rationale = (
            f"Your initiative scored {scored.composite_score:.2f}/10. "
            "Based on current portfolio capacity and strategic priorities, "
            "we are unable to commit resources at this time."
        )
        next_steps = (
            "1. Re-submission is welcome in 90 days if business conditions change\n"
            "2. Review the scoring rationale above to identify areas for strengthening the case\n"
            "3. Consider whether this initiative can be partially addressed by an existing approved project"
        )
    return DECISION_TEMPLATE.format(
        request_id=scored.request.request_id,
        requestor_name=scored.request.requestor,
        title=scored.request.title,
        decision=decision,
        priority_tier=tier,
        score=scored.composite_score,
        rationale=rationale,
        next_steps=next_steps,
        pm_name=pm_name,
        pm_email=pm_email,
        tech_lead=tech_lead,
        ticket_url=ticket_url,
    )

The intake portal itself generates valuable data about the health of the AI programme. Tracking intake metrics reveals whether the organisation is generating too many low-quality requests, experiencing capacity bottlenecks, or seeing demand concentrated in a narrow range of use cases.
Investigate if drops suddenly — signals disengagement
% requests decided within 7 BD of submission
Add triage capacity or streamline criteria
Recalibrate scoring if >20% — too many false P1s
Review communication templates and rationale quality
Form too long: intake forms with 20+ fields have low completion rates. Prune ruthlessly to the eight fields that drive the triage decision.
No SLA enforcement: without a published and monitored SLA, triage queues back up, requestors lose confidence, and shadow projects proliferate.

Opaque scoring: requestors who do not understand why their request was deferred will resubmit the same request repeatedly in frustration. Always include the score breakdown in decision communications.

P1 inflation: if every escalating stakeholder gets their request fast-tracked, the fast-track tier loses meaning and the standard queue is never worked. Maintain a strict scoring threshold for P1 regardless of seniority.
A demand intake portal makes all AI requests visible and comparable, preventing the loudest stakeholder from dominating programme capacity.
Automated triage — completeness checking, duplicate detection, risk tier assignment — should complete within one hour of submission, with human review following within three business days.
A weighted composite scoring model (value, alignment, urgency, effort, risk) produces defensible priority decisions that requestors can understand and accept.
Four priority tiers with defined SLAs, routing paths, and resource commitments turn scores into operational commitments.
Intake portal metrics measure programme health: track submission volume, decision speed, and requestor satisfaction alongside the portfolio KPIs.
